title: Folk art bird papercut
subtitle: A beginner papercutting pattern for a stylised bird silhouette in the central-European folk tradition
excerpt: A stylised bird with a curved body, a fan tail, and simple feather cuts is one of the most common motifs across European papercutting traditions. This pattern works from a template, introduces interior cutting with a scalpel, and produces a silhouette that can be mounted on card or in a frame.
sourceNotes: Folk bird motif common across Polish, Ukrainian, German, and Scandinavian papercutting traditions. The silhouette form and feather-cut details are widely documented in craft and folk-art literature. This entry synthesises common formal elements.

glossaryTerms.definition: The areas of paper removed by cutting. In papercutting, the design is defined as much by its negative space as by the silhouette that remains. Skilled papercutters place negative cuts to create visual detail (feather barbs, petal veins) that would not be possible in a solid silhouette.

A fold made in the template paper before cutting to align the template accurately with the folded or layered working paper. The registration fold ensures the design is centred or correctly placed before any cutting begins.

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Work with a fresh scalpel blade for interior cuts: a dull blade drags and causes the paper to tear rather than cut. Keep the non-cutting hand well clear of the blade's path. For interior cuts, pierce the paper at the centre of the shape to be removed and cut outward toward the outline, not toward your hand.

content.text: The bird silhouette uses two types of cut: outline cuts with scissors (the external body shape) and interior cuts with a scalpel (the feather detail and any openwork in the body and tail). The interior cuts are
that give the bird texture and visual weight. Without them, the silhouette is flat; with them, it reads as a detailed illustration.

Draw or trace the bird outline on plain paper. The body should fit within an approximately 10 x 8 cm rectangle: large enough to cut cleanly, small enough to complete in one session. The body curve runs from the beak at the left, rounds over the back, and drops to a fan tail at the right. The tail fans outward to the right, 3 to 4 cm wide.

Mark the interior feather cuts on the template: a series of narrow V-cuts or thin slots along the wing line and across the tail fan. These will be your guide for scalpel cutting. Mark the eye as a small circle to be cut out entirely.
in the template along the bird's belly line if you intend to fold the working paper for a mirrored pair of birds. For a single bird, no fold is needed.

Trace or transfer the outline onto the working paper using a light box or by rubbing pencil on the back of the template and pressing through. Cut the interior scalpel cuts first, before the outer outline: it is easier to hold the paper flat for fine cuts while the sheet is still intact.

For each interior feather cut, make two short inward cuts forming a V or slot. Lift the cut piece with the scalpel tip rather than fingers; very small cut pieces are easier to remove with the blade tip than to pinch.
Once all interior cuts are complete, cut the outer outline with scissors. Work in a smooth, continuous curve where possible: scissors produce a cleaner edge than a scalpel on convex outer shapes.

Lay the finished bird face-down and apply small spots of PVA to the edges. Mount on a contrasting sheet of card. Cover with blotting paper and press under a book for 30 minutes to prevent the bird from curling as the adhesive dries.
